turns.content: how do you study for an exam when you physically do not have time to study for an exam. asking for myself
That's a frustrating position to be in. What exam is it, and when is it? Knowing the timeline and subject will help me figure out what's actually realistic.
its my ACA exam. march 18th. so two weeks from now basically. and i am nowhere near ready

Two weeks for an ACA exam is tight but not impossible — it depends on how much ground you need to cover. Which paper is it? And when you say nowhere near ready, do you mean you haven't started revising at all, or you've done some but don't feel confident?

ive done some but its been so patchy. like i did a chunk of revision in january but then busy season kicked in and ive barely touched it since. i do maybe 30 minutes here and there on the weekend but thats it. its the advanced audit paper and theres SO much content. i keep telling myself ill catch up but i never do because im exhausted from work

OK so you're not starting from zero — you have a January foundation, it's just gone cold. That's recoverable. The advanced audit paper is scenario-heavy, which actually works in your favour because you're literally doing audit work every day right now. A lot of the practical knowledge is already in your head from work, you just need to translate it into exam technique.

Here's a two-week rescue plan:

**Week 1 (now to March 11):**
- Focus on past papers, not textbook revision. Do at least one full past paper under timed conditions this weekend
- After each paper, mark it and identify your weak topics — those are what you revise, not everything
- Weeknight sessions: 30-45 minutes of targeted revision on weak areas only

**Week 2 (March 11-17):**
- Two more timed past papers
- Focus on exam technique: time allocation, answer structure, hitting the marking points
- Light revision only in the final two days — don't cram, consolidate

How many hours per day can you realistically commit to revision right now?

honestly during the week maybe 45 minutes to an hour in the evening if i force myself. weekends i can probably do 3-4 hours on saturday and then sunday ive got family stuff but maybe 2 hours in the evening. its not a lot. the problem is im doing 12-14 hour days at work and by the time i get home my brain is completely gone. like i open the textbook and the words just swim

Those hours add up more than you think. Let me do the maths:

- Weeknights: 45 min x 10 evenings = ~7.5 hours
- Saturdays: 3.5 hours x 2 weekends = 7 hours
- Sundays: 2 hours x 2 weekends = 4 hours
- **Total: ~18.5 hours**

18 hours of focused, targeted revision is enough to pass if you use it efficiently. The key word is "targeted" — you cannot afford to waste any of those hours re-reading things you already know.

For the weeknight brain-dead problem: try doing revision in the morning instead. Set your alarm 45 minutes early, revise before work when your brain is fresh. I know mornings are hard, but evening revision when you're fried from a 14-hour day is barely productive. Would you consider flipping it?

i literally hate mornings so much but youre right that trying to revise at 10pm is pointless. i read the same paragraph 4 times and retain nothing. ok fine. ill try the morning thing. wallah if i have to set 5 alarms i will. this exam is important, if i fail it sets me back 6 months

The 6-month setback is real motivation. And honestly, two weeks of early mornings is a short sacrifice for not having to resit. Some tips to make the mornings work:

- **Prepare the night before** — have your revision materials open and ready on your desk. Reduce the activation energy to zero
- **Start with practice questions, not reading** — active recall wakes your brain up faster than passive reading
- **Coffee first, then questions** — give yourself 10 minutes to wake up before diving in

Also — have you talked to your manager about study leave? Most firms give you at least a few days off before ACA exams.

yeah i have study leave booked for the thursday and friday before the exam so march 13 and 14. and then the exam is on the tuesday. so thats technically 4 days including the weekend. but knowing busy season my manager might try to pull me back in and i will have to physically fight him on it because that study leave is sacred

Guard that study leave with everything you have. If your manager tries to pull you back, remind him that the firm literally requires you to pass these exams — it's in their interest too. PwC's ACA pass rates are something they report on; nobody benefits from you failing because you couldn't revise.

Those 4 days (Thursday, Friday, Saturday, Sunday) before the Tuesday exam are gold. Plan them now:

- **Thursday:** Full past paper in the morning, review and targeted revision in the afternoon
- **Friday:** Another past paper, focus on timing and technique
- **Saturday:** Final past paper + revision of any remaining weak spots
- **Sunday:** Light review only. Consolidate notes, go through key formulas/frameworks. Rest in the evening
- **Monday:** Do nothing exam-related. Genuinely rest. Go into Tuesday fresh

Does your firm provide any revision courses or resources?

yeah we have access to Kaplan study materials and there are some recorded revision lectures i havent watched yet. i should probably prioritise those over the textbook actually since theyre designed around the exam format. ok im writing all this down. thursday friday saturday sunday. past papers. morning revision before work starting tomorrow. this is doable. i think. maybe. inshallah

It is doable. The Kaplan revision lectures are a good call — they're condensed and exam-focused, which is exactly what you need at this stage. Much more efficient than going back through the full textbook.

Priority order for your remaining time:
1. **Past papers** (most important — this is where you learn exam technique)
2. **Kaplan revision lectures** (efficient content review)
3. **Targeted textbook revision** (only for specific weak topics identified from past papers)

Skip anything you already know well. No comfort revision — it feels productive but wastes time. Every minute should be spent on things you're genuinely unsure about.

comfort revision is literally what ive been doing loool. just re-reading the chapters i already understand because it feels good. ok no more of that. weak areas only. right. i feel slightly less panicked now which is good because 20 minutes ago i was genuinely considering whether i could fake my own death to avoid taking this exam

Faking your own death would probably involve more paperwork than the exam itself, so I'd say revision is the easier route.

Seriously though — the fact that you have a January foundation, work experience that directly maps to the exam content, study leave booked, and Kaplan materials available means you have more going for you than it feels like right now. Two weeks of focused effort is all you need. You've got this. March 18th, get it done, then you never have to think about this particular paper again.

the paperwork comment is sending me loool. ok im actually going to go do some revision right now while im feeling motivated. thank you wallah you've talked me off the ledge. march 18th. its happening. pray for me

Go smash it. Open the Kaplan lectures, do some questions, and ride this wave of motivation while it's here. And when March 18th comes, you'll walk in more prepared than you think. Good luck — you've got this.
